channel.title: Project Management on RaihnForge
channel.description: Recent content in Project Management on RaihnForge

item.title: From Intuition to Intention: My Journey in Project Management
item.description:
Prepared for ADMG 479, Project Management Maturity, for Professor Dr. Laura Williamson. Drafted by Joshua Keyes, June 4, 2025.

Introduction

My journey through the Project Management specialization at Central Washington University has been one of steady growth, practical application, and transformative learning. With a diverse professional background spanning design, compliance, and team leadership, I entered this program with a desire to formalize my management experience and refine my skills in organizational planning, execution, and strategic communication. This reflection paper serves as a capstone for that passage, offering a comprehensive reflection on how this specialization shaped my growth as a project manager.

item.title: ITAM Project Management Internship Reflection
item.description:
1. Learning Objectives

My Spring 2025 internship at an EdTech employer focused on three core learning objectives that guided my development as a systems-oriented project manager and visual communicator. Building on foundational experiences from ITAM 490A, I sought to translate theory into practice by enhancing tool proficiency, streamlining operational workflows, and elevating cross-team communication through visual storytelling.

Objective 1 — Become Proficient with Jira

I deepened my knowledge of Jira by managing sprint cycles, grooming backlogs, and setting up project-specific dashboards. These systems were implemented across both the internal content-system product pipeline and the Upper Elementary microlesson content initiative. I structured Epics and Stories to reflect evolving production workflows, enabling greater task traceability and faster throughput. Dashboards became key tools in daily team syncs, reinforcing accountability and aligning output with strategic objectives.

item.title: Agile Manifesto Values and Principles
item.description: The Agile Manifesto provides a foundational framework for agile project management, emphasizing adaptability, collaboration, and a customer-focused approach. It comprises four core values and twelve principles. Below, each is described and contextualized with examples from personal project experience. An acronym for AGILE proposed by Roula Lombardi is Adapt continually, Groom your requirements and refinements for release of features, Iterate through two-to-four-week scrum cycles, Launch and learn through deployment, and Empower your scrum teams (Lombardi, 2020).

item.title: Agile Process Terms
item.description:
Product Owner

The Product Owner represents the customer or stakeholder and is responsible for prioritizing and refining the Product Backlog to deliver the most value. As a project manager, the supporting role is to ensure backlog clarity, facilitate prioritization, and align deliverables to stakeholder goals. The challenge is managing evolving priorities while maintaining a clear development path.

Agile Project Manager / Scrum Master

This role facilitates Agile processes, removes blockers, and ensures the team adheres to Agile principles. As a project manager, the work is to act as a servant-leader: supporting team autonomy, encouraging collaboration, and keeping momentum steady. The challenge is balancing traditional project leadership with Agile facilitation without overstepping into micromanagement.

item.title: SMART Goals Progress Report
item.description:
This is a mid-quarter status report on three personal SMART goals, written as a project-manager email to my instructor. Before the report itself I want to note something about the assignment structure, because part of the skill of professional communication is recognizing when the format gets in the way of the message.

A Note on the Assignment Format

A real project manager rarely updates on three unrelated goals in one email. The progress report template in the textbook is built for one project, with one set of milestones, one set of anticipated problems, and one infographic. Stacking three goals into a single template creates an abstraction layer that does not match how a supervisor actually digests progress. The cleaner version of this assignment is one report per goal, three reports total, in priority order. The cleaner version of the infographic is one per goal as well. I am submitting the combined version because that is the assignment, but I want to name the awkwardness on the record. Recognizing when the template is fighting the audience is part of the communication skill the class is teaching.

item.title: ITAM Internship A Reflection
item.description:
1. Learning Objectives

My first learning objective was to gain experience as an Agile project-management scrum master for the development team. Though I assist in prioritization and help unblock the development team throughout sprints, our team does not ascribe to strict scrum implementation. I also prefer to avoid the scrum points system, as it creates a distraction and an alluring incentive for team members to prioritize the gamified system over the actual applied outcomes. My objective was modified to increase confidence and organization during developer daily standups and bi-weekly meetings. I incorporated weekly one-on-one meetings with individual developers in order to assess and assist with progress and prioritization.

item.title: Internship Week 02: Contribution to the Company
item.description:
1. Contribution to the Company

As a project manager at the EdTech employer, I oversee SOC 2 Type 2 certification and lead a website redesign initiative. The SOC 2 Type 2 work is moving slowly but ramping up as we commit to audit dates in collaboration with our SOC 2 auditor. The website redesign has entered phase two. Phase one’s deliverable was confirming that the current site was up to date, compliant, and information-accurate. The redesign itself requires graphic-design collaboration with our design team, and because I have a graphic-design background I was chosen to execute it. The work is being done in Figma, a new tool for me, since past web designs only required Adobe Photoshop. I continue to work on these initiatives, though I have not yet been able to commit the time and attention I would prefer.

item.title: Teaming: Tuckman's Model and the Agile / Waterfall Split
item.description:
This article will discuss the shared management and development of high-performance teams in relation to Tuckman’s Model for Nurturing a Team to High Performance. The model has five clear stages or factors: Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, and Adjourning. The important factors to building a high-performance agile team and a traditional waterfall project management approach team can be subtly different in focus of characteristics desired in team members.

Five Factors

The five stages of Tuckman’s Model were developed by a psychologist named Bruce Tuckman in 1965 (Mind Tools Content Team, 2023). The concept aligns closely with other team forming theories such as the US Army’s stages of team building: formation, development, and sustainment (Portolese et al, 2018). Both the US Army model and Tuckman’s Model coincide with the team-building stages defined in The Art of Leadership and Supervision as Formation Stage, Enrichment Stage, and Sustainment Stage (Portolese et al, 2018). The following outlines Tuckman’s Model in more depth.

item.title: Managing Crisis Projects
item.description: An organization must be accountable, responsive, and integrate understanding in a crisis situation for appropriate management. It is important to understand the difference between risks and unknowns and how to approach their management differently (Paladin Risk, 2014). Project risk denotes factors that may benefit or negate the viability of maintaining the project’s baseline and successfully completing deliverables within constraints, most often scope, cost, and schedule (Meyer et al, 2002). Unknowns are defined as unforeseeable occurrences (Darnall et al, 2018). Unforeseen circumstances may still be addressed through proper understanding of crisis management (Paladin Risk, 2014). Crisis management is defined as “a significant threat to operations that can have negative consequences if not handled properly” (Coombs, 2007). Failing to consider these factors and how they correlate to the life-cycle phases of a crisis may determine that the organization are villains in the eyes of the public instead of the victims of a crisis (Kerzner, 2017). This discernment may determine the fate of an organization.

item.title: Risk Management Plan: Nurse Workstation Refresh
item.description:
Notional client project. “Central Medical Center” and “Dr. Phylicia Patterson” are fictional names from the assignment brief. The risk identification approach, register, matrix, and response strategies below are my own applied work, presented here lightly edited from the course deliverable.

Risk Management Plan

Risk management is a necessary aspect to review due to the potential aspects of the project that may deviate to varying degrees from expected outcomes. These possibilities are primarily associated with the core constraints of the project, and the degree of deviation from those constraints is what constitutes the risk factor. The core constraints, in priority order, are time, scope, quality, and cost.

item.title: Master Project Management Plan: Nurse Workstation Refresh
item.description:
Notional client project. “Central Medical Center” and “Dr. Phylicia Patterson” are fictional names from the assignment brief. The project mechanics, schedule, WBS, and management plans below are my own applied work, presented here lightly edited from the cumulative course deliverable.

Introduction

The Nurse Workstation Refresh Phase 1 is a technology refresh necessary to maintain high quality standards, reduce future maintenance costs, increase security, and increase efficiency for the stakeholders of Central Medical Center. Conducting Phase 1 of the greater initiative will begin the facility-wide refresh, prioritizing increased quality to areas that represent the first-line interaction with clients. 101 nurse workstations on floors 3, 4, and 5 will be updated to Windows 10. This document captures the costs, timelines, and scope necessary to achieve project success, with the clarity in their concept, execution, and control that the organization expects.

item.title: Project Management Plan: Nurse Workstation Technology Refresh — Phase 1
item.description:
This is the cumulative ADMG 474 project management plan, written in the voice of the Project Manager for Central Medical Center (Ellensburg, WA). It is presented here lightly edited for the portfolio. Tables, budget figures, and matrix attachments are referenced in the original but not reproduced in this web view.

Introduction

The Nurse Workstation Refresh Phase 1 is a technology refresh that is necessary to maintain high quality standards, reduce future maintenance costs, increase security, and increase efficiency for the stakeholders of Central Medical Center. Conducting Phase 1 of the greater initiative will begin the facility-wide refresh, prioritizing increased quality to areas that represent our first-line interaction with clients. 101 nurse workstations on floors 3, 4, and 5 will be updated to the more current operating system, Windows 10. Included in this document are details in support of a clear understanding of constraints for Nurse Workstation Refresh Phase 1. Costs, timelines, and scope are crucial elements of achieving project success. The clarity in their concept, execution, and control are a priority and this document communicates the understanding of each within the organization.

item.title: Final Individual Reflection: Team Project Performance
item.description:
This is the end-of-quarter reflection for ADMG 374. The team delivered a nonprofit website project across three labs and a final, with four members working entirely over Microsoft Teams and Zoom during the COVID quarter. The team average came out at 99.7. I scored the team at 96, reserving room for improvement that any team can use.

Team Performance

Teammate 1 was excellent at the work itself, and was open about a schedule pulling them in many directions. Their risk management section in Lab 3 carried the depth of someone who had taken a dedicated risk class the prior quarter, and it raised the ceiling of the deliverable.

item.title: Communicating About and Managing Risk
item.description:
This paper responds to Schwalbe Chapters 10 and 11. Chapter 10 covers communication. Chapter 11 covers risk. They are paired for a reason: most of what we call risk in projects is, underneath, a communication failure that nobody named in time.

Chapter 10: Communication

Tone of Voice

Oral communication carries information that words alone do not (Schwalbe, 2015, p. 394). Someone can say “I am fine with the timeline” and be telling you something different with their voice. Stress and tension show up in tone before they show up in vocabulary, often before the speaker has even registered the feeling themselves. A project manager who only reads the words is reading half the message.

item.title: COVID Uplift: Team PM Plan, Progress, Staff, Quality, Risk
item.description:
Editor’s note: classmate names in the original document have been replaced with Teammate 1, Teammate 2, and Teammate 3 in keeping with this archive’s privacy conventions.

This is the Lab 3 mid-flight artifact for the ADMG 374 team project. Teammate 2 rotated into the Project Manager role for this lab; I co-authored across the four planning sections and was the assigned risk owner for the highest-impact item on the qualitative matrix. The artifact bundles the triple-constraint progress report, the staffing management plan with RACI, the quality management plan, and the risk management plan into a single Lab 3 submission.

item.title: COVID Uplift: Team Project Charter
item.description:
Editor’s note: classmate names in the original document have been replaced with Teammate 1, Teammate 2, and Teammate 3 in keeping with this archive’s privacy conventions.

This is the kickoff artifact for the ADMG 374 team project. Four students worked entirely over Microsoft Teams and Zoom across the COVID winter quarter to deliver a hypothetical nonprofit website serving Kittitas County. I was the named Project Manager on this charter. The charter bundles the project charter proper, the business case, a stakeholder analysis, and the team contract, all signed off at the front of the quarter.
